elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a Markov-functional approach to construct local volatility models that are calibrated to a discrete set of marginal distributions.
elements.sent-2411-002: The method is inspired by and extends the volatility interpolation of Bass (1983) and Conze and Henry-Labord\`{e re (2022).}
elements.sent-2411-003: The method is illustrated with efficient numerical algorithms in the cases where the constructed local volatility functions are: (1) time-homogeneous between or (2) continuous across, the successive maturities.
elements.sent-2411-004: The step-wise time-homogeneous construction produces a parsimonious representation of the local volatility term structure.
elements.sent-2411-005: The interpolation and extrapolation of a two-dimensional implied volatility surface is known to be a non-trivial problem.
elements.sent-2411-006: For a partial list of desirable properties one wishes to preserve in the construction of implied volatility surface, see Carr (2010) \cite{carr2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: The solutions fall into two broad categories: the best-fit approach by a parametric model (e.g., Heston, SABR) and the non-parametric approaches.
elements.sent-2411-008: For the non-parametric approaches, previous attempts are made in two ways: (1) a two-step approach of fitting of an implied volatility skew in strike dimension followed by an interpolation in the maturity dimension \cite{carr2010, andreasen2011, lipton2011, phl2019 ; (2) a full-scale global approach where a non-parametric representation is fitted directly to all observed option prices \cite{avellaneda1997,verma1999 .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Dupire (1994) \cite{dupire1994 , Derman and Kani (1994) \cite{derman1994 in a discrete-time setting, show that it is possible to construct a local volatility model that is consistent with a full surface of European option prices as a function of maturity and strike, while, in practice, only a discrete set of which is observed.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we consider the problem of volatility interpolation in the maturity dimension, given a discrete set of one-dimensional marginals.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our approach is inspired by and extends the construction of local volatility model of Bass (1983) \cite{bass1983 and Conze and Henry-Labord\`{e re (2022) \cite{phl2022 , calibrated exactly to one or more marginals.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The step-wise time-homogeneous construction described in Section \ref{krylov_section produces a parsimonious representation of the local volatilities.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Mimicking certain features of an It\^{o process by the solution of a stochastic differential equation (SDE) is a recurring topic of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Krylov (1985) \cite{krylov1985 constructs a time-homogeneous diffusion that possesses the same Green $\lambda$-measure as a general It\^{o process, where a Green $\lambda$-measure of a process records the average amount of time that the process spends in each Borel set before being killed at the first jump of an independent Poisson process with intensity $\lambda$.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Gy\"{o ngy (1986) \cite{gyongy1986 proves that there exists a time-dependent diffusion process with non-random coefficients that matches the one-dimensional marginal distributions of an It\^{o process.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Brunick and Shreve (2013) \cite{shreve2013 extend Gy\"{o ngy (1986) by showing that the mimicking process can preserve not only the marginal distributions, but also the joint distribution of certain functionals of the It\^{o process (e.g., running maximum, running average) at each fixed time.}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: More recently, Lacker, Shkolnikov, and Zhang (2019) \cite{lacker2019 inverts the Markovian projection of Gy\"{o ngy (1986) \cite{gyongy1986 by finding a different It\^{o process with the fixed-time marginal distributions matching those of a given one-dimensional diffusion.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Another line of work is devoted to a similar problem: mimicking an It\^{o process that is a sub-martingale by a Markov process, not restricted to the solutions of SDE.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Kellerer (1972) \cite{kellerer1972 establishes the existence of the Markov solutions matching pre-specified marginals, extended to multiple dimensions by Pammer, Robinson, and Schachermayer (2023) \cite{pammer2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Madan and Yor (2002) \cite{madan2002 provide explicit constructions of such Markov processes using three methods: Skorokhod embedding, time-changed Brownian motion by inhomogeneous independent increments, and continuous construction by Dupire (1994) \cite{dupire1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-022: In Section \ref{method , we introduce the framework of Markov-functional models with local drift, its specializations and fixed-point numerical algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In Section \ref{example , we illustrate the methods by two sets of examples: one case with synthetic double exponential distributions and another with market data.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In Section \ref{conclusion , we summarize and conclude.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In Appendix \ref{skew_fitting , we describe the method we used to fit the implied volatility skews, hence the marginal distributions, slice by slice such that no-arbitrage conditions are enforced in both the strike dimension and the maturity dimension.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Consider a horizon $T$ and a discrete set of marginal distributions $\nu_i$ on $\mathbb{R}$, $1\le i\le n$, implied in practice from market prices of vanilla options with maturities $0\triangleq T_0 < T_1 < \cdots < T_n \triangleq T$ and free of calendar arbitrage.
elements.sent-2411-027: To construct a (real-valued) martingale price process $(S_t)_{0\le t\le T}$, that meets the given marginal distributions, we assume that $S_t$ can be written as a Markov functional \cite{bergomibook : \begin{equation}\label{flowfunction} S_t = f(t,X_t),\end{equation} where $(X_t)_{0\le t\le T}$ is a process driven by a Brownian motion $(W_t)_{0\le t\le T}$ and guided by a local drift function $\mu(t,x)$: \begin{equation}dX_t = \mu(t,X_t)dt + dW_t, \quad X_0\in R.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The forward density $p(t,x)$ of $X_t$ is known to solve the Fokker-Planck equation: \begin{equation}\label{ppde} \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\mu - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\right]p(t,x) = 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-029: Applying It\^{o 's formula to $f(t,X_t)$ yields: \begin{equation}\label{itolemma} df(t,X_t) = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + \mu \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}+ \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}\right)dt + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} dW_t.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{itolemma} df(t,X_t) = \left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + \mu \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}+ \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}\right)dt + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} dW_t.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-030: We gather from Eq.(\ref{itolemma ) and the martingale property of $S_t$ that $f(t,x)$ satisfies: \begin{equation}\label{fpde} \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \mu \frac{\partial}{\partial x} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\right]f(t,x) = 0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-031: To borrow a terminology from probabilistic modeling and inference, we call the process $(X_t)_{0\le t\le T}$ the flow variable and the function $f(t,x)$ the flow function.
elements.sent-2411-032: Correspondingly, the process $S_t$ is called the target variable and $\nu_1,\cdots,\nu_n$ the target distributions.
elements.sent-2411-033: Given a drift function $\mu(t,x)$ and the initial conditions \begin{equation}p(0,x) = \delta(x-X_0), \quad f(0,X_0) \equiv S_0,\end{equation} where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac-$\delta$ function, one can solve Eq.(\ref{ppde ) and Eq.(\ref{fpde ) under appropriate boundary conditions or terminal conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: At $T_0=0$, the flow function $f(0,x)$ typically depends on the specific form of the model, e.g., the drift function $\mu(t,x)$.
elements.sent-2411-036: The resulting dynamics of $S_t=f(t,X_t)$ is a local volatility model: \begin{equation}\label{localvolandflow} dS_t = \partial_x f(t,f^{-1}(t,S_t))dW_t,\end{equation} where $\partial_x f (t,x)\triangleq \frac{\partial f {\partial x (t,x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: According to Eq.(\ref{quantilematching ), the flow function at a marginal maturity is monotonically increasing, i.e., $\partial_x f(T_i,x)\ge 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In fact, any local volatility model specified by a local volatility function $\sigma_{\text{loc (t,\cdot)$ can be written in an equivalent form of a flow function $f(t,x)$ determined by \begin{equation}\label{flowfunctionode} \frac{\partial f(t,x)}{\partial x} = \sigma_{loc}(t,f(t,x)) \Rightarrow \int \frac{df}{\sigma_{loc}(t,f)} = x.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Comparing Eq.(\ref{flowfunctionode ) with the BBF formula of Berestycki, Busca, and Florent \cite{bbf2004 gives the following implicit equation for the Markov flow function $f(0,\cdot)$ at $t=0$: \begin{equation}\label{bbfrelation} \frac{f-S_0}{\sigma_{imp}(T=0,f)} = x-X_0,\end{equation} if the short-term implied volatility skew $\sigma_{\text{imp (T=0,\cdot)$ is sufficiently regular.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In general, Eq.(\ref{quantilematching ), a relation between the flow function and the flow variable's distribution function, will not be satisfied by an arbitrary drift function $\mu(t,x)$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Instead, the drift function $\mu(t,x)$ (hence the forward distributions of the flow variable) and the flow function $f(t,x)$ need to solved jointly.
elements.sent-2411-042: A simple example of the flow function known in closed-form is the Black-Scholes model, where $f(t,x) = S_0 \exp\left( \sigma x - \frac{1 {2}\sigma^2 t \right)$ and $(X_t)_{0\le t\le T}$ is a Brownian motion.. {\setlength{\parindent {0cm Remark.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: If $(X_t)_{0\le t\le T}$ and $f(t,x)$ is a solution to Eq.(\ref{flowfunction ), then with any deterministic function $C(t)$ of time $t$, $\tilde{X _t \triangleq X_t + C(t)$ and $\tilde{f (t,x) \triangleq f(t, x - C(t))$ is also a solution.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This ambiguity can be removed by enforcing $\mathbb{E [X_t]=0$, $\forall t\in[0,T]$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Bass (1983) \cite{bass1983 considers a single forward period $t\in[0,T_1)$, where the flow variable $X_t$ is a Brownian motion itself, with $X_0=0$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In other words, the drift function $\mu(t,x)\equiv 0$.
elements.sent-2411-047: In the first forward period $t\in[0,T_1)$, the distribution of the flow variable at time $t$ is simply a Gaussian $X_t\sim \mathcal{N (0,t)$: \begin{equation}F_{X_{t}}(x) = N\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{t}}\right), \quad t\in[0,T_1),\end{equation} where $N(\cdot)$ is the CDF of the standard normal distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-048: By quantile-matching, the flow function at $t=T_1$ is determined: \begin{equation}f(T_1,x) = F^{-1}_{\nu_1}\circ N\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{T_1}}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: Because $\mu(t,x)\equiv 0$, the flow function satisfies a heat equation $\frac{\partial f {\partial t + \frac{1 {2}\frac{\partial^2 f {\partial x^2 = 0$ for $t\in[0,T_1)$ and is a convolution of its terminal values at $T_1$ with the heat kernel propagator from $T_1$ back to $t$, \begin{equation}\label{bass_quadrature_1} f(t,x) = K(T_1,t)\star f(T_1,x) \triangleq \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} K_{T_1-t}(y)f(T_1,x-y)dy,\end{equation} where $K_\tau(x) \triangleq \frac{1 {\sqrt{2\pi \tau e^{-\frac{x^2 {2\tau }$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The above procedure is illustrated in Figure (\ref{first_period_chart ).}
elements.sent-2411-051: Note that, in the continuous-time limit of an appropriate discretization of the target distribution in binomial quantiles, the implied binomial tree of Rubinstein (1994) \cite{rubinstein1994 corresponds to the Bass martingale $f(t,W_t)$, $t\in[0,T_1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The solution is known as the standard stretched Brownian motion.
elements.sent-2411-053: Conze and Henry-Labord\`{e re (2022) \cite{phl2022 extends the construction of Bass (1983) \cite{bass1983 to the subsequent forward periods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For each forward period $t\in [T_i, T_{i+1 )$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n-1$, the flow variable $X_t$ still has Brownian dynamics $dX_t = dW_t$, but in this case combined with an uncertain initial value $X_{T_i}$.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The probability distribution of the initial value $X_{T_i}$ is determined jointly with the flow function for the period $t\in [T_i, T_{i+1 )$ by the following fixed-point equation: \begin{equation}\label{phl_quadrature_1} F_{X_{T_i}} = F_{\nu_i}\circ K(T_{i+1},T_i) \star F_{\nu_{i+1}}^{-1}\circ K(T_i, T_{i+1})\star F_{X_{T_i}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{phl_quadrature_1} F_{X_{T_i}} = F_{\nu_i}\circ K(T_{i+1},T_i) \star F_{\nu_{i+1}}^{-1}\circ K(T_i, T_{i+1})\star F_{X_{T_i}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-056: The flow function for $t\in [T_i, T_{i+1 )$ is obtained afterwards: \begin{equation}\label{phl_quadrature_2} f(t,x) = K(T_{i+1},t) \star F_{\nu_{i+1}}^{-1}\circ K(T_i, T_{i+1})\star F_{X_{T_i}}(x).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\label{phl_quadrature_2} f(t,x) = K(T_{i+1},t) \star F_{\nu_{i+1}}^{-1}\circ K(T_i, T_{i+1})\star F_{X_{T_i}}(x).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: The above procedure is illustrated in Figure (\ref{second_period_chart ) for $t\in[T_1,T_2)$:.}
elements.sent-2411-058: More recently, Acciaio et al. (2023) \cite{acciaio2023 shows the existence and uniqueness of the solution to Eq.(\ref{phl_quadrature_1 ) and prove that this model converges to the Dupire local volatility model when the mesh size $\sup_{i=1,\cdots,n |T_i-T_{i-1 |$ converges to zero.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Joseph et al. (2023) provides a clear exposition on the link between the fixed-point iteration of Eq.(\ref{phl_quadrature_1 ) and the measure-preserving martingale Sinkhorn's system.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The flow variable in Bass (1983) \cite{bass1983 and Conze and Henry-Labord\`{e re (2022) \cite{phl2022 has Brownian dynamics, which facilitates an efficient simulation of Monte Carlo paths.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Moreover, the flow functions in the successive forward periods $[T_{i-1 ,T_{i )$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n$, can be solved in parallel.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: In particular, the distributions of the flow variable at the beginning of each forward period are determined separately.
elements.sent-2411-063: As a consequence, the overall flow function (hence the local volatility function) is, in general, not continuous across the marginal maturities.
elements.sent-2411-064: In a Monte Carlo simulation, the flow variable $X_{T_i}$ at the beginning of each forward period $[T_i,T_{i+1 )$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n-1$, must be path-wisely determined by the continuity condition: \begin{equation}\label{continuity1} S_{T_i} = S_{T_{i}^-} \Rightarrow X_{T_i} = f^{-1}(T_i, f(T_i^-, X_{T_i^-})).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\label{continuity1} S_{T_i} = S_{T_{i}^-} \Rightarrow X_{T_i} = f^{-1}(T_i, f(T_i^-, X_{T_i^-})).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-065: Finally, the flow function in this construction is explicitly time-dependent, which may lead to unrealistic term structures in the local volatility interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-066: We construct a step-wise time-homogeneous (i.e., autonomous) diffusion process, in the spirit of Krylov (1985) \cite{krylov1985 , that, instead of replicating the Green $\lambda$-measure, meets a discrete set of marginal distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-067: In the sense of a generalized diffusion, the existence of a time-homogeneous local volatility model in $t\in[0,T]$ that meets a given marginal at $t=T$ is established by Noble (2013) \cite{noble2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Our approach is to start with a time-homogeneous flow function: \begin{equation}S_t = f(X_t),\end{equation} where the flow variable has a time-homogeneous drift $\mu(x)$: \begin{equation}dX_t = \mu(X_t)dt + dW_t.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: The dynamics of $S_t$ is a manifestly time-homogeneous local volatility model: \begin{equation}dS_t = f'(f^{-1}(S_t))dW_t,\end{equation} where $f'(x)\triangleq \frac{df(x) {dx}$, $f''(x)\triangleq \frac{d^2f(x) {dx^2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The martingale property of $S_t$ given in Eq.(\ref{fpde ) imposes the following relation between the flow function and the drift function: \begin{equation}\label{krylovdrift} \mu(x) = - \frac{f''(x)}{2f'(x)} = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial }{\partial x}\log f'(x), \quad if \,\, f'(x)>0.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The drift function $\mu(x)$ set by Eq.(\ref{krylovdrift ) is undefined when $f'(x)=0$ or $\infty$, which occurs when the probability density of the flow variable at $x$, or the target variable at $f(x)$, respectively, is exactly zero.}
elements.sent-2411-072: For Algorithm \ref{krylov_fixed_point_1 and \ref{krylov_fixed_point_2 to work properly, we impose the following condition on the target distributions: \begin{assumption In Algorithm \ref{krylov_fixed_point_1 and \ref{krylov_fixed_point_2 , the target marginal cumulative distribution functions $F_{\nu_i (\cdot)$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n$, are strictly increasing on $[0,\infty)$ or $(-\infty,+\infty)$.}}}}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Time-homogeneous flow fixed-point iteration for $t\in[0,T_{1})$}\label{krylov_fixed_point_1} \begin{algorithmic} \State Start with an initial guess of $\mu(x)$ \Repeat \State Solve the forward equation (\ref{ppde}) to obtain $F_{X_{T_1}}(x)$. \State Set the flow function by $f(x) = F_{\nu_1}^{-1}\circ F_{X_{T_1}}(x)$. \State Update drift function $\mu(x) = -\frac{f''(x)}{2f'(x)}$. \Until{$\mu(x)$ and $f(x)$ converge.} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-073: For each subsequent forward period $t\in[T_i,T_{i+1 )$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n-1$, we construct a step-wise time-homogeneous flow function.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Similar to Conze and Henry-Labord\`{e re (2022) \cite{phl2022 , the flow variable $X_t$ has an uncertain initial value $X_{T_i}$ whose probability distribution needs to be determined jointly with the drift function $\mu(x)$ and the flow function $f(x)$ according to the condition that the flow function is time-homogeneous.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Based on the fact that there is only one flow function $f(x)$ in each period, the fixed-point equation for $F_{X_{T_i (x)$ reads \begin{equation}F_{X_{T_i}}(x) = F_{\nu_i}\circ F_{\nu_{i+1}}^{-1}\circ \hat{K}^{\text{ {forward}},\mu}_{T_i\rightarrow T_{i+1}} \left[F_{X_{T_i}} (x)\right].\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The following joint fixed-point equation produces such a solution when the iteration converges (Algorithm \ref{krylov_fixed_point_2 ):.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The construction of a Krylov-style local volatility model described in this section trades the same degrees of freedom in each marginal distribution with a time-homogeneous local volatility function in the corresponding forward period: \begin{equation}dS_t = \sigma_i(S_t)dW_t, \quad t\in[T_{i-1},T_i),\end{equation} where $i=1,2,\cdots,n$.
elements.sent-2411-079: It has the benefit of avoiding unrealistic shapes in the local volatility interpolation in the dimension of maturity, but still generates discontinuities across market maturities.
elements.sent-2411-080: In a Monte Carlo simulation, the flow variable $X_{T_i}$ at the beginning of each forward period $[T_i,T_{i+1 )$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n-1$, must be determined path-wisely by the continuity condition: \begin{equation}\label{continuity2} S_{T_i} = S_{T_i^-} \Rightarrow X_{T_i} = f_{[T_i,T_{i+1})}^{-1}\circ f_{[T_{i-1},T_i)}(X_{T_i}^-),\end{equation} where $f_{[T_i,T_{i+1 ) (\cdot)$ denotes the time-homogeneous flow function restricted to the forward period $[T_i,T_{i+1 )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: A specification of term structure underlies every volatility interpolation scheme considered previously.
elements.sent-2411-082: In Section \ref{bass_section and \ref{phl_section , the term structure is implicitly encoded in the heat kernel propagator $K_\tau(x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In Section \ref{krylov_section , the term structure is explicitly specified to be step-wise time-homogeneous.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Local volatility surfaces constructed period by period $[T_{i-1 ,T_i]$ to meet the marginal distributions at $T_i$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n$, are typically discontinuous across the marginal maturities.}
elements.sent-2411-085: To enforce continuity, one solution is to explicitly specify a term structure of the flow function that is continuous in time and across maturities, by interpolating the snapshots $f(T_i,\cdot)$ at the marginal maturities $T_i$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n$.
elements.sent-2411-086: For example, assume that $f(t,x) = a_i + \frac{b_i {\sqrt{t }$, $\forall t\in [T_{i ,T_{i+1 ]$, for constant $a_i$ and $b_i$, \begin{equation}\label{singularterm} f(t,x) = \frac{\sqrt{\frac{T_i T_{i+1}}{t}}-\sqrt{T_i}}{\sqrt{T_{i+1}}-\sqrt{T_i}}f(T_i,x) + \frac{\sqrt{T_{i+1}}-\sqrt{\frac{T_i T_{i+1}}{t}}}{\sqrt{T_{i+1}}-\sqrt{T_i}}f(T_{i+1},x),\end{equation} where the snapshots $f(T_i,x)$ are, as usual, determined by the quantile-matching condition in Eq.(\ref{quantilematching ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: At $t=0$, the flow function is typically undetermined except the requirement that $f(0,X_0)=S_0$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Although the term structure given by Eq.(\ref{singularterm ) is singular at $t=0$, it serves as a valid flow function at $t=0$ as long as $f(0,X_0)=S_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The specific form of the flow function term structure is a modeling choice dependent on the relationship between the flow variable and the target variable.
elements.sent-2411-090: For example, if the flow variable is a Brownian motion $W_t$ and the target distribution is log-normal, a linear interpolation in the logarithm of the flow function is more appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-091: The martingale property of $S_t$ given in Eq.(\ref{fpde ) imposes the following relation between the flow function and the drift function: \begin{equation}\label{timelineardrift} \mu(t,x) = -\frac{\partial_t f(t,x)+ \frac{1}{2}\partial_x^2 f(t,x)}{\partial_x f(t,x)},\end{equation} where the time derivative $\partial_t {f (t,x)\triangleq \frac{\partial f {\partial t (t,x)$ can be evaluated according to the specified time-dependence of $f(t,\cdot)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The following fixed-point equation produces such a solution when the iteration converges (Algorithm \ref{continuous_flow_fixed_point ):.}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{Continuous flow fixed-point iteration for $t\in[T_{i},T_{i+1})$}\label{continuous_flow_fixed_point} \begin{algorithmic} \State Input: $F_{X_{T_i}}(x)$ and $f(T_i,x)$. \State Start with an initial guess of $\mu(x)$. \Repeat \State Solve the forward equation (\ref{ppde}) to obtain $F_{X_{T_{i+1}}}(x)$. \State Set the flow function $f(T_{i+1},x)$ at maturity $T_{i+1}$ by Eq.(\ref{quantilematching}). \State Interpolate the flow function, for example, according to Eq.(\ref{singularterm}). \State Update drift function by Eq.(\ref{timelineardrift}). \Until{$f(T_{i+1},x)$ converge.} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-093: Subject to the condition that the fixed-point iteration converges, the resulting solution produces a continuous local volatility function across the marginal maturities.
elements.sent-2411-094: In a Monte Carlo simulation, there is no need for matching the flow variable over successive forward periods by the continuity condition such as Eq.(\ref{continuity1 ) or Eq.(\ref{continuity2 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-095: However, the term structure of the resulting local volatility function is often oscillatory.
elements.sent-2411-096: Consider an underlying volatility model $\sigma_t$ and the construction of a local volatility model $\sigma_{\text{loc (t,\cdot)$ matching the marginal distributions at $T_i$ as well as the corresponding variance swaps $\int_{T_{i-1 ^{T_{i }\mathbb{E [\sigma_t^2] dt$, for $i=1,2,\cdots,n$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Unless $\mathbb{E [\sigma_{\text{loc ^2(t,\cdot)]$ matches up with $\mathbb{E [\sigma_t^2]$ exactly for $\forall t\in[T_0,T_1]$, an undershooting (overshooting) at $t=T_1$ inevitably leads to an overshooting (undershooting) at $t=T_2$ and an oscillatory term structure over $T_2,T_3,\cdots,T_n$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: To illustrate the local volatility constructions described previously, we consider two sets of test cases: one case with a synthetic family of double exponential marginal distributions and another with market options data.
elements.sent-2411-099: In the latter case, a discrete set of option prices is observed for each maturity.
elements.sent-2411-100: Before applying the Markov-functional method to interpolate volatility in the maturity dimension, we perform a parametric fitting of the implied volatility skews, hence the risk-neutral distributions, for each market maturity.
elements.sent-2411-101: The method of skew fitting is described in Appendix \ref{skew_fitting .}
elements.sent-2411-102: We first consider the family of double negative exponential densities for the target variable $S_t=Y_t$: \begin{equation}\label{doublexponentialdensity} p(t,y) = \frac{\lambda(t)}{2}e^{-\lambda(t)|y|},\end{equation} where $\lambda(t)$ is a positive decreasing function of time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-103: For example, we choose $\lambda(t)=\frac{1 {\sqrt{t }$ as in Madan and Yor, 2002 \cite{madan2002 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: In this model, the prices of vanilla options, the local volatility function (Bachelier-type), the marginal CDF and its inverse are all known in closed form.

elements.sent-2411-135: In Figure (\ref{double_exponential_compare ), we compare the local volatility term structures of the continuum model (dashed lines) and those generated by interpolation schemes.}

elements.sent-2411-137: The oscillation is much reduced for $y=1,2,3$.
elements.sent-2411-138: The time-homogeneous model (dash-dotted lines) generates the most parsimonious, monotonic but discontinuous, term structure.
elements.sent-2411-139: Because the continuum model given by Eq.(\ref{doubleexponentiallocalvolsurface ) is singular at $t=0$ for $y\neq 0$, the continuous interpolation (dotted lines), or any other model with a finite local volatility at $t=0$, overshoots at the beginning of the second period $[T_1,T_2]$, forcing it to undershoot at the end of the same period, to match the overall volatility level (or equivalently total variance) of the continuum model.}
elements.sent-2411-140: This explains the alternating slopes of the term structure given by the continuous model, characteristic of a calibrated local volatility surface.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Market Options Data: JPM, as of February 15, 2023
elements.sent-2411-142: The second test case we consider is the market options data of JPMorgan on February 15, 2023, shown in Figure (\ref{jpm_data ).}
elements.sent-2411-143: For each strike, a de-Americanization procedure is performed to compute a Black implied volatility that matches up with the corresponding American option price.
elements.sent-2411-144: Also shown in Figure (\ref{jpm_data ) is the parametric fitting of the implied volatility skew at each maturity to a 4-mode mixed lognormal (MLN) risk neutral density.}
elements.sent-2411-145: The procedure to avoid calendar spread arbitrage in the slice-by-slice fitting of the implied skews is detailed in Appendix \ref{skew_fitting .}
elements.sent-2411-146: With the calibrated target distributions at $T\in\{2, 30, 65, 121, 156, 212\}$ in units of days, we report the results of the step-wise time-homogeneous volatility interpolation algorithms in Section \ref{krylov_section .}
elements.sent-2411-147: For brevity, the results for the continuous Markov function described in Section \ref{continuous_section are not reported.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Constructing a continuous Markov function by interpolating the snapshots at $T_i$, $i=1,2,\cdots,n$, generally produces an oscillatory term structure --- an issue made worse by the irregularities in the market options data.
elements.sent-2411-149: To fix the scale, the target variable $S_t$, the JPMorgan price, as well as the option strikes are normalized by the forward.
elements.sent-2411-150: The initial value of the target value $S_0=1$.
elements.sent-2411-151: We choose the flow variable to have an initial condition $X_0=0$.

The same set of step-wise time-homogeneous local volatility functions are shown in Figure (\ref{jpm_krylov_localvol}) (right panel) in one graph.} \label{fig:jpm_compare_bass_krylov}
elements.sent-2411-167: This paper develops an alternative perspective of viewing local volatility models --- the Markov functional model (MFM) \cite{bergomibook .}
elements.sent-2411-168: In this framework, a local volatility model can be specified in two equivalent ways: (1) the local volatility function $\sigma_{\text{loc (t,y)$; (2) the flow function $f(t,x)$ and the drift function $\mu(t,x)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: The linkage among the three functions, as well as the risk-neutral density $p(t,y)$ of the target variable, is shown in Figure (\ref{threewaylink ):.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Given a discrete set of marginal distributions, i.e., partial information about $p(t,y)$, the Markov functional method leads to the construction of local volatility models of different characteristics, for example, step-wise time-homogeneous local volatility functions or those continuous in time and across marginal maturities.
elements.sent-2411-171: The resulting local volatility models can generate different implied skews for intermediate maturities.
elements.sent-2411-172: Instead of imposing a term structure of the flow function a priori, selecting a drift function or a flow function in an optimal sense merits further study.
elements.sent-2411-173: An extension of the Markov functional approach beyond the local volatility models, e.g., path-dependent volatility models, is an interesting topic for future research.. \begin{appendices.}
elements.sent-2411-174: The volatility interpolation procedure developed in this paper depends on the availability of the marginal distribution functions $F_{\nu_i (\cdot)$ to perform quantile-matching at the marginal maturities, as in Eq.(\ref{quantilematching ).}}
elements.sent-2411-175: To apply the interpolation procedure to market options data, an arbitrage-free (butterfly or calendar) fitting of the implied volatility skews is critical.
elements.sent-2411-176: Fitting implied skews slice by slice separately, however, suffers from calendar arbitrage, causing the subsequent volatility interpolation in the dimension of maturity to fail.
elements.sent-2411-177: This is shown in the left panel of Figure (\ref{jpm_fit ) for JPM options data on February 15, 2023, where one can see some crossings in the total implied variances of different maturities.}
elements.sent-2411-178: One idea to remove the calendar arbitrage is to (1) use the Andreasen \& Huge (A\&H) local volatility \cite{andreasen2011 to enforce the convex order between successive marginal distributions; (2) perform an mixed lognormal (MLN) fit on the solution of the A\&H scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-179: The procedure is described in Algorithm \ref{mln_ah .}
elements.sent-2411-180: The resulting fit of implied skews is shown in the right panel of Figure (\ref{jpm_fit ), where the crossings in total implied variance are removed at the expense of slightly reduced quality of fitting for some slices at large moneyness.}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[scale=0.35]{jpm_options_data_fit_v2.pdf} \caption{Mixed lognormal fit to JPM options data on February 15, 2023. Left panel: fitting each slice of implied skews separately. Right panel: fitting the slices sequentially according to Algorithm \ref{mln_ah}.} \label{jpm_fit}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{Mixed Lognormal Fit of Andreasen \& Huge (A\&H) Solutions}\label{mln_ah} \begin{algorithmic} \State $\bullet$ Start from the skew at $T_0$: $C(T_0,K) = (S_0-K)^+$. \State $\bullet$ For $i=0,1,2,\cdots$, assume the full skew $C(T_i,K)$ is available. \State $\bullet$ Fit an MLN model $C^{\text{MLN}}(T_{i+1},K)$ to the prices $C(T_{i+1},K_j)$ at $T_{i+1}$. \State $\bullet$ Compute A\&H local vol using $C(T_i,K)$ and $C^{\text{MLN}}(T_{i+1},K)$: \begin{equation}\theta^2_i(K) \triangleq \frac{\frac{C^{MLN}(T_{i+1},K)-C(T_i,K)}{T_{i+1}-T_i}}{\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{\partial^2 C^{MLN}(T_{i+1},K)}{\partial K^2}}.\end{equation} \State $\bullet$ Solve the one-step implicit scheme of the forward PDE: \begin{equation}\left[ 1 - \frac{1}{2}(T_{i+1}-T_i)\theta^2_i(K)\frac{\partial^2}{\partial K^2} \right]\hat{C}(T_{i+1}, K) = C(T_{i}, K)\end{equation} \State $\bullet$ Optimize the MLN model parameters with respect to \begin{equation}\min_{MLN parameters} \sum_{j} | C(T_{i+1}, K_j) - \hat{C}(T_{i+1}, K_j) |^2.\end{equation} \State $\bullet$ Obtain the solution of the one-step forward PDE as $C(T_{i+1},K)$. \State $\bullet$ Fit an MLN model to $C(T_{i+1},K)$ on the PDE grid. \end{algorithmic}
